topics.title: Definition and Importance; Comparison with Traditional Rigid Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT751 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition and Importance; Comparison with Traditional Rigid Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Underwater Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PERUT751 / Module 1)**
> **Underwater Robotics** is a specialized branch of robotics engineering that deals with the design, construction, operation, and application of robotic systems capable of performing tasks in submerged or partially submerged aquatic environments (freshwater, seawater, or brine). It integrates principles from **mechanical engineering, electrical engineering, ocean engineering, control theory, computer science, and marine biology** to develop autonomous or remotely operated vehicles that can sense, plan, and act beneath the water surface.

The field primarily encompasses three classes of robotic platforms:

1. **Remotely Operated Vehicles (ROVs)** — Tethered robotic systems controlled in real-time by a human operator on a surface vessel via an umbilical cable that transmits power, control signals, and data.
2. **Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs)** — Untethered, self-powered robotic platforms that execute pre-programmed missions with onboard intelligence, navigation, and decision-making capabilities.
3. **Hybrid Remotely Operated Vehicles (HROVs)** — Dual-mode vehicles that can switch between tethered (ROV mode) and autonomous (AUV mode) operation, e.g., WHOI's *Nereus* and *Orpheus* class.

> [!NOTE]
> **Auxiliary Terminology (KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary)**
> - **UUV** — Unmanned Underwater Vehicle (umbrella term for AUVs and ROVs)
> - **USV** — Unmanned Surface Vehicle (used as a communication relay)
> - **ROV/AUV Manipulator** — A rigid robotic arm mounted on an underwater vehicle for intervention tasks (also a key focus of this module).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Underwater Smart Car"

> [!TIP]
> **Think of an underwater robot as a "smart car" that drives through water instead of roads.** A traditional car (rigid robotic arm in a factory) works in a clean, predictable environment with known obstacles, fixed coordinates, and reliable grip. An underwater robot is more like an **off-road Mars rover that has been dunked into the ocean** — it must deal with:
> - **High pressure** (like being squished by a stack of elephants as you go deeper)
> - **Zero GPS** (radio waves die in salt water within meters)
> - **Poor visibility** (murky water, like driving in thick fog at night)
> - **Strong, unpredictable currents** (the road itself is moving)
> - **Corrosion** (the salt water slowly dissolves unprotected metal)
> - **Communication blackout** (you can only "talk" to it via sound waves, very slowly)

So underwater robotics is essentially **"robotics made harder by Mother Nature"** — every design decision must account for a hostile, uncertain, and dynamic fluid environment.

---

## 1.3 Why Is Underwater Robotics Important? (KTU 2024 — Engineered Importance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the world needs underwater robots:** More than **80% of Earth's ocean remains unmapped and unexplored**. Human divers face severe physiological limits (depth, decompression sickness, hypothermia). Underwater robots are the only practical means to perform sustained, repeatable, and safe work in the ocean depths.

| Domain | Specific Importance | Typical Depth / Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Scientific Research** | Mapping seafloor topography, studying marine biology, sampling hydrothermal vents | 0 m – 11,000 m (full ocean depth) |
| **Offshore Oil & Gas** | Inspection of risers, pipelines, subsea manifolds, valve actuation | 50 m – 3,000 m |
| **Defense & Security** | Mine counter-measures (MCM), harbor protection, intelligence gathering | 10 m – 600 m |
| **Search & Recovery** | Locating black boxes, downed aircraft, shipwrecks | Variable |
| **Environmental Monitoring** | Oil spill tracking, coral reef health, pollution assessment | 0 m – 6,000 m |
| **Cable & Pipeline Survey** | Laying and inspecting trans-oceanic telecom cables | 10 m – 6,000 m |
| **Aquaculture** | Net inspection, fish stock counting, automated feeding | 5 m – 50 m |

> [!NOTE]
> **A canonical industry statistic (frequently cited in KTU 2024 references):**
> A single deep-sea ROV dive can cost **USD 30,000 – 100,000+ per day**, so reliability, autonomy, and intelligent fault recovery are non-negotiable engineering requirements.

---

## 1.4 Definition — "Traditional Rigid Robotics" (For Comparison)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Traditional Rigid Robotics (TRR)** refers to the classical industrial / manipulator-class robotic systems characterized by:
> - **Rigid links** (typically made of steel or aluminum) connected by **revolute or prismatic joints**
> - **High structural stiffness** with minimal deflection under load
> - **Fixed-base** or **mobile-base** operation in **structured, terrestrial (air) environments**
> - **High-precision position control** (sub-millimeter accuracy achievable)
> - **Predictable dynamics** governed by well-defined equations of motion (Euler-Lagrange or Newton-Euler)
> - Examples: 6-DOF industrial arms (KUKA, ABB, FANUC), SCARA robots, Cartesian gantries, mobile robots on factory floors.

These robots are governed by mature, well-established mathematics (Denavit-Hartenberg parameters, Jacobian-based control, computed torque, etc.) and operate in environments where:
- **Gravity is constant and known**
- **Atmospheric pressure is fixed at ~101 kPa**
- **No buoyancy effects on the base or payload**
- **Visual sensing is reliable (cameras, LiDAR work perfectly)**
- **Wireless communication (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth) is available**

Underwater robotic manipulators are a *hybrid* of these two worlds: they have the rigid-link kinematics of a traditional arm, but the **base** is a free-floating underwater vehicle subject to all the harshness described in §1.2.

---

## 1.5 Section 1 — Key Takeaway

> [!TIP]
> **One-line mental model:**
> *Underwater Robotics = Rigid-Link Robotics + Hostile Fluid Environment + Limited Sensing/Communication + Severe Power/Pressure Constraints.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix

## 2.1 Why a Side-by-Side Comparison Matters in KTU Examinations

The KTU 2024 scheme examiner (as per the official Module 1 syllabus) deliberately tests the student's ability to **contrast** the design philosophy of underwater robots against the rigid-link manipulators they studied in earlier semesters (e.g., in *Robotics and Automation* or *Mechatronics* courses). The examiner wants you to demonstrate that you understand **WHY** classical robotics theory cannot be directly transplanted underwater — it must be **adapted**.

## 2.2 The Six Pillars of Comparison (Analytical Framework)

We compare the two paradigms across six engineering axes:

### Pillar 1 — **Operating Environment**

| Aspect | Traditional Rigid Robotics | Underwater Robotics |
|---|---|---|
| Medium | Air (homogeneous, transparent) | Water (dense, often turbid, sometimes opaque) |
| Pressure | ~**101 kPa** (constant) | **101 kPa + 10 kPa/m of depth** (linear with depth) |
| Gravity | Constant downward ($g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$) | Effective weight reduced by buoyancy ($F_B = \rho V g$) |
| Temperature | Stable | Variable (thermal layers, thermoclines) |
| Corrosion | Negligible | Severe (saltwater corrosion) |

### Pillar 2 — **Mechanical Structure & Dynamics**

| Aspect | Traditional Rigid Robotics | Underwater Robotics |
|---|---|---|
| Base | Fixed to ground / moving on a known plane | Free-floating, 6-DOF vehicle subject to **hydrodynamic forces** |
| Link stiffness | Very high (steel/aluminum) | Same, but **hydrodynamic drag** adds distributed loads |
| Mass properties | Inertia tensor $I$ is constant | Inertia tensor can change with **payload / water absorption** |
| Joint friction | Well-modeled (viscous + Coulomb) | Joints are **sealed** → additional friction from O-rings and packing |
| External forces | Predictable (gravity, contact) | **Added mass**, **drag**, **buoyancy**, **current forces** dominate |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Favorite Concept — "Added Mass":**
> When a body accelerates through a fluid, it must also accelerate a portion of the surrounding fluid. This appears as an **added mass** term $m_a$ in the equation of motion, increasing the effective inertia:
> $$m_{eff} = m_{body} + m_{added\ mass}$$
> This phenomenon has **no real counterpart** in terrestrial rigid robotics and is a frequent 14-mark question topic.

### Pillar 3 — **Actuation & Power**

| Aspect | Traditional Rigid Robotics | Underwater Robotics |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | Mains electricity (unlimited) | Onboard batteries (limited) **or** surface via tether |
| Actuator type | Electric servo motors (dominant), hydraulic, pneumatic | Thrusters (waterproof brushless DC), hydraulic manipulators, pressure-balanced oil-filled systems |
| Power density | High | **Battery energy density is the #1 limiting factor** for AUVs |

### Pillar 4 — **Sensing & Perception**

| Aspect | Traditional Rigid Robotics | Underwater Robotics |
|---|---|---|
| Vision | Cameras, LiDAR — high resolution, real-time | Cameras with **artificial illumination**; range is **< 5 m** in clear water |
| Distance | LiDAR, IR, ultrasonic | **Sonar (acoustic) is dominant** — multi-beam, side-scan, sub-bottom |
| Position | GPS, fiducial markers, motion capture | **No GPS underwater** → uses DVL (Doppler Velocity Log), INS, USBL/LBL acoustic positioning |
| Inertial | IMU (works perfectly in air) | IMU in a **pressure housing**, often combined with DVL for dead reckoning |

### Pillar 5 — **Communication**

| Aspect | Traditional Rigid Robotics | Underwater Robotics |
|---|---|---|
| Wireless | Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, 5G — fast, reliable | **Acoustic modems** — slow ($\sim$**kbps**), high latency, multi-path |
| Wired | Fieldbus, Ethernet (fast) | Tether on ROVs (power + data), but tether management is a major engineering challenge |
| Latency | < 1 ms | Acoustic: **0.5 – 2 seconds round-trip** for 1 km distance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Speed of Sound vs. Radio:**
> Sound travels in water at ~**1500 m/s**; radio waves at ~$3 \times 10^8$ m/s. This 5-orders-of-magnitude difference fundamentally changes control loop design — you **cannot** close a high-bandwidth feedback loop over an acoustic link.

### Pillar 6 — **Control & Autonomy**

| Aspect | Traditional Rigid Robotics | Underwater Robotics |
|---|---|---|
| Control paradigm | Position control, trajectory tracking, force/impedance control | Often **velocity control** with integral action; **sliding-mode** and **adaptive control** are popular for hydrodynamic uncertainty |
| Model fidelity | Very high (rigid-body dynamics) | Lower (hydrodynamic coefficients are hard to identify exactly) |
| Autonomy level | Pre-programmed routines, vision-based pick-and-place | Full mission autonomy: path planning in unknown terrain, obstacle avoidance, fault detection, energy-aware decision making |

---

## 2.3 KTU 2024 Formula Sheet — Underwater vs. Rigid Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are the most frequently tested formulas for this topic in KTU 2024 ESE.**

| Symbol / Concept | Equation | Units | Where it Appears |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hydrostatic pressure | $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ | Pa | Depth-related design |
| Buoyant force | $F_B = \rho_{water} \cdot V_{displaced} \cdot g$ | N | Static stability of ROV/AUV |
| Apparent weight | $W_{apparent} = (\rho_{body} - \rho_{water}) \cdot V \cdot g$ | N | Net downward force on submerged body |
| Drag force (quadratic) | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A v^2$ | N | Thruster sizing, trajectory planning |
| Added mass force | $F_{added} = m_a \dot{v}$ | N | Dynamics of submerged body |
| Speed of sound in water | $c \approx 1500$ m/s | m/s | Acoustic modem / sonar design |
| Round-trip acoustic delay (range $R$) | $t_{RT} = 2R / c$ | s | Communication latency budget |
| Denavit-Hartenberg (rigid arm) | $A_i = Rz(\theta_i) \cdot Tz(d_i) \cdot Tx(a_i) \cdot Rx(\alpha_i)$ | — | Manipulator kinematics (same as rigid robotics) |
| Equation of motion (rigid) | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ | — | Standard rigid robot dynamics |
| Equation of motion (submerged rigid arm) | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G_{apparent}(q) + F_{hydro} = \tau$ | — | Modified for buoyancy + drag |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP — Don't write the equation of motion as the same for both!**
> The underwater version has **two extra terms**:
> 1. $G_{apparent}$ — gravity reduced by buoyancy
> 2. $F_{hydro}$ — hydrodynamic forces (drag + added mass)
> Forgetting either of these = **at least 2–3 marks lost** in a 14-marker.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the comparison matters in practice:**
> 1. **Design Reuse** — Engineers can reuse DH parameters, Jacobian, and inverse kinematics from rigid robotics for the manipulator arm; they only need to redesign the *base dynamics* and *control laws*.
> 2. **Simulation** — Tools like MATLAB/Simulink, Gazebo, and UWSim (Underwater Simulator) are built upon rigid-body dynamics libraries, extended with hydrodynamic plugins.
> 3. **Standardization** — Many subsea-manipulator manufacturers (e.g., Schilling Robotics, Subsea Tech, ECA Group) sell *modified* industrial arms (like a Titan 4 or Orion) into pressure-balanced oil-filled housings, retaining the original rigid-link kinematics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Comparisons

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Apparent Weight Under Water (Why Submerged Objects Feel Lighter)

**Problem Setup:** A rigid steel cube of side $L = 0.5$ m, density $\rho_{steel} = 7850$ kg/m³, is fully submerged in seawater ($\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³). Compute the apparent weight.

### Step 1 — Compute the body's mass
$$m = \rho_{steel} \cdot V = 7850 \cdot (0.5)^3 = 7850 \cdot 0.125 = 981.25\ \text{kg}$$

*Logic:* Mass is an intrinsic property of the body, independent of the surrounding fluid.

### Step 2 — Compute the actual (in-air) weight
$$W_{air} = m \cdot g = 981.25 \cdot 9.81 = 9626.06\ \text{N}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the buoyant force (Archimedes' Principle)
$$F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot V \cdot g = 1025 \cdot 0.125 \cdot 9.81 = 1256.91\ \text{N}$$

*Logic:* The displaced water volume equals the cube's volume since it is fully submerged.

### Step 4 — Compute the apparent (in-water) weight
$$W_{apparent} = W_{air} - F_B = 9626.06 - 1256.91 = 8369.15\ \text{N}$$

### Step 5 — Express the result as a ratio
$$\frac{W_{apparent}}{W_{air}} = \frac{8369.15}{9626.06} \approx 0.869$$

**Interpretation:** Even dense steel feels about **13% lighter** when fully submerged. For lightweight materials (e.g., aluminum with $\rho = 2700$ kg/m³), the apparent weight is reduced even more, sometimes making the object effectively **buoyancy-neutral** or even **positively buoyant** (floats). This is precisely why ROVs use syntactic foam and buoyancy modules.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydrostatic Pressure as a Function of Depth

**Problem:** Compute the absolute pressure experienced by an ROV operating at a depth of $h = 2000$ m.

### Step 1 — Identify parameters
- Surface pressure: $P_0 \approx 101325$ Pa
- Density of seawater: $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³
- Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81$ m/s²

### Step 2 — Apply the hydrostatic equation
$$P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

### Step 3 — Substitute values
$$P = 101325 + 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 2000$$

$$P = 101325 + 20,110,500 = 20,211,825\ \text{Pa}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to bar for intuition
$$P \approx 202.1\ \text{bar}$$

**Interpretation:** The ROV's pressure housing must withstand **~200 atmospheres** of external pressure. This is the primary reason why the structural design of deep-sea robots is so different from a factory robot's steel chassis.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Drag Force on a Spherical Underwater Sensor

**Problem:** A spherical sensor of diameter $D = 0.1$ m moves through seawater at $v = 1.5$ m/s. Compute the drag force. Take $C_D = 0.47$ (typical for a smooth sphere at moderate Reynolds number) and $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.

### Step 1 — Compute the cross-sectional area
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Apply the quadratic drag equation
$$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A v^2$$

### Step 3 — Substitute
$$F_D = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 0.47 \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \cdot (1.5)^2$$

$$F_D = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 0.47 \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 2.25$$

### Step 4 — Compute the product
$$F_D \approx 4.26\ \text{N}$$

**Interpretation:** Even at 1.5 m/s (a slow swimming pace), the drag on a 10 cm sphere is ~4 N — enough to require careful thruster sizing. For an AUV's larger hull moving at 3–4 m/s, drag forces scale with the **square of velocity** and become dominant.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Acoustic Communication Latency

**Problem:** An AUV is surveying the seafloor at a horizontal range of $R = 3$ km from a surface ship. Compute the one-way and round-trip acoustic latency.

### Step 1 — Use the speed of sound in seawater
$$c \approx 1500\ \text{m/s}$$

### Step 2 — One-way delay
$$t_{one-way} = \frac{R}{c} = \frac{3000}{1500} = 2.0\ \text{s}$$

### Step 3 — Round-trip delay
$$t_{RT} = \frac{2R}{c} = 4.0\ \text{s}$$

**Interpretation:** A 4-second round-trip means that **classic closed-loop position control is impossible** over an acoustic link. This is the fundamental reason AUVs carry **onboard autonomy** — they cannot rely on a human operator teleoperating in real time. This is in stark contrast to an industrial robot where control loops run at **1 kHz** with sub-millisecond latency.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Numerical Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Board Question Format (Use this matrix verbatim if asked.)**

| Parameter | Industrial Rigid Arm (6-DOF, KUKA-like) | Underwater Manipulator on ROV |
|---|---|---|
| Operating medium | Air | Seawater |
| Environmental pressure | 101 kPa | Up to **20+ MPa** at 2000 m |
| Gravity contribution to torque | Full $g$ | Reduced by buoyancy (often **<30%** of in-air weight) |
| Base dynamics | Fixed / wheeled base | **6-DOF floating base** with current disturbance |
| Dominant external force | Gravity, contact force | **Drag + added mass + current** |
| Communication latency to controller | < 1 ms (bus) | **2–10 s** over acoustic link |
| Position control bandwidth | ~100 Hz | ~1–5 Hz (limited by sensors & hydrodynamics) |
| Repeatability | ±0.02 mm (best in class) | ±5–50 mm (state of the art) |
| Typical energy per hour | kW from mains | **~1 kWh from battery** (4–8 hour mission) |
| Failure cost | Production downtime | Loss of multi-million dollar vehicle on seafloor |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board "Compare and Contrast" Answer Template:**
> Begin every comparison answer with:
> *"While both systems share the same fundamental rigid-link kinematics (DH parameters, Jacobian, etc.), they differ fundamentally in their base dynamics, external disturbance models, sensing modalities, and control architectures because of the contrasting operating environments."*
> This single opening sentence can earn **1–2 easy marks**.

---

## 3.6 Quick Numerical Sanity Check (For Exam Confidence)

| Scenario | In Air | Submerged in Seawater | Ratio (submerged / air) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Apparent weight (steel cube) | 9626 N | 8369 N | 0.87 |
| Drag (sphere at 1.5 m/s) | ~0.5 N | ~4.3 N | ~8.6× |
| Pressure at 2 km depth | 101 kPa | 20,212 kPa | 200× |
| Comm. latency (3 km range) | $\mu$s (radio) | 4 s (acoustic) | $\sim 10^6 \times$ |

These ratios are **frequently asked** as "estimate the order of magnitude" questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — Taxonomy of Underwater Robots

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram showing the high-level classification of underwater robotic systems, mapping every major concept discussed in Sections 1–3.**

```mermaid
graph TD
    root1[Underwater Robotics]
    root1 --> cat1[UUV Unmanned Underwater Vehicle]
    root1 --> cat2[USV Unmanned Surface Vehicle]
    root1 --> cat3[Subsea Manipulator Systems]

    cat1 --> uuv1[ROV Remotely Operated Vehicle]
    cat1 --> uuv2[AUV Autonomous Underwater Vehicle]
    cat1 --> uuv3[HROV Hybrid Vehicle]

    uuv1 --> rov1[Observation Class ROV]
    uuv1 --> rov2[Work Class ROV]
    uuv1 --> rov3[Heavy Work Class ROV]

    uuv2 --> auv1[Man Portable AUV]
    uuv2 --> auv2[Lightweight AUV REMUS Class]
    uuv2 --> auv3[Heavyweight AUV Hugin Class]
    uuv2 --> auv4[Glider buoyancy driven]

    cat3 --> man1[Hydraulic Manipulator]
    cat3 --> man2[Electric Manipulator Oil Filled]
    cat3 --> man3[Compliant Gripper]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — Comparison Architecture Flow (Rigid vs. Underwater)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This block-level architecture flow maps the functional subsystems of a traditional rigid robot versus an underwater robot, highlighting where the design philosophies diverge.**

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph TRAD[Traditional Rigid Robotics]
        T1[Power Mains AC DC] --> T2[Controller PLC or Industrial PC]
        T2 --> T3[Servo Drivers]
        T3 --> T4[Rigid Link Manipulator]
        T4 --> T5[End Effector Tool]
        T6[Encoders IMU] --> T2
        T7[Camera LiDAR] --> T2
        T8[Ethernet WiFi] --> T2
    end

    subgraph UND[Underwater Robotics]
        U1[Onboard Battery or Surface Tether] --> U2[Onboard Computer or Surface PC]
        U2 --> U3[Thruster Drivers ESC]
        U3 --> U4[Floating Vehicle Hull]
        U4 --> U5[Manipulator Arm with Joints]
        U5 --> U6[Gripper or Tool]
        U7[INS DVL Pressure Sensor] --> U2
        U8[Sonar and Camera with Lights] --> U2
        U9[Acoustic Modem or Tether] --> U2
        U10[Pressure Housing and Buoyancy Modules] --> U4
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block 3 — Control Loop Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Side-by-side control loop diagram showing why underwater control is fundamentally harder.**

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CR[Classical Rigid Control Loop]
        A1[Desired Trajectory q d] --> A2[Controller 1 kHz]
        A2 --> A3[Servo Amplifier]
        A3 --> A4[Robot Joints]
        A4 --> A5[Encoder Feedback]
        A5 --> A2
    end

    subgraph CU[Underwater Control Loop]
        B1[Desired Pose] --> B2[Onboard Controller 1 to 10 Hz]
        B2 --> B3[Thruster and Manipulator Drivers]
        B3 --> B4[Submerged Vehicle and Arm]
        B4 --> B5[Sonar DVL INS Feedback]
        B5 --> B2
        B6[Acoustic Link 2 to 10 s latency] -.-> B2
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block 4 — Workflow for Designing an Underwater Robot (Block Architecture)

> [!TIP]
> **The end-to-end functional flow used by engineers when designing an underwater robotic system. Often tested in 14-mark KTU questions as a "describe the design methodology" type question.**

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Mission Objective Definition] --> P2[Operational Depth and Environment]
    P2 --> P3[Vehicle Type Selection ROV or AUV or HROV]
    P3 --> P4[Pressure Hull Design]
    P3 --> P5[Buoyancy and Ballast Design]
    P3 --> P6[Thruster Configuration and Maneuvering]
    P3 --> P7[Sensor Suite Selection]
    P3 --> P8[Manipulator Selection and Integration]
    P4 --> P9[Power and Energy Budget]
    P5 --> P9
    P6 --> P9
    P7 --> P9
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Control and Autonomy Stack]
    P10 --> P11[Communication Architecture]
    P11 --> P12[Testing in Lab Pool and Open Water]
    P12 --> P13[Deployment and Mission Execution]
```
