| Sensor Noise | Turbulence, EMI | Kalman filtering, sensor fusion | Computational load | **High** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Three-Pillar Challenge Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Underwater Robot System] --> B[Pillar 1: Durability]
    A --> C[Pillar 2: Control Precision]
    A --> D[Pillar 3: Integration]

    B --> B1[Pressure Hull]
    B --> B2[Seals and Penetrators]
    B --> B3[Corrosion Protection]
    B --> B4[Biofouling Defense]
    B --> B5[Battery Endurance]

    C --> C1[Nonlinear Drag]
    C --> C2[Cross Coupling]
    C --> C3[Acoustic Latency]
    C --> C4[Sensor Noise]
    C --> C5[INS Drift]

    D --> D1[Hardware Interfaces]
    D --> D2[Power Bus EMI]
    D --> D3[Software Middleware]
    D --> D4[Thermal Management]
    D --> D5[Payload Swapping]

    B1 --> F[Mission Failure Risk]
    B3 --> F
    C3 --> F
    C4 --> F
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    F --> G[Design Trade Study]
    G --> H[Optimised AUV or ROV]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Feedback Loop with Underwater-Specific Disturbances

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Waypoint] --> S[Summing Junction]
    S --> C[PI or PID Controller]
    C --> A[Thruster Actuator]
    A --> P[Vehicle Dynamics Gps]
    P --> O[Output Position and Attitude]

    O --> FB[Feedback Sensor: DVL, INS, USBL]
    FB --> S

    P -. Disturbance .-> P
    D1[Ocean Current] -.-> P
    D2[Tether Tension for ROV] -.-> P
    D3[Wave Surface Forcing] -.-> P

    C -. Limited by .-> L1[Acoustic Latency]
    C -. Limited by .-> L2[Sensor Bandwidth]
    C -. Limited by .-> L3[Actuator Saturation]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Integration Stack (Layered Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 5: Mission Planner and GUI] --> L2[Layer 4: Autonomy and Decision Engine]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Real Time Vehicle Controller QNX VxWorks]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 2: Middleware ROS2 DDS CAN Ethernet]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 1: Hardware Abstraction Drivers and Sensors]
    L5 --> L6[Layer 0: Pressure Hull Thrusters Batteries]

    L6 --> X[Integration Challenge: Each layer must be tested, certified, and version-locked]
    L4 --> X
    L2 --> X
    X --> Y[System Level HIL Simulation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The first diagram shows the *taxonomy* of the three pillars and their sub-challenges converging on mission failure. The second shows *where* disturbances enter a control loop and *which bottlenecks* cap precision. The third illustrates the *layered software stack* where integration mismatches most frequently occur — a real KTU 14-mark question often asks the student to annotate such a diagram with failure modes.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

*List any three challenges of underwater robotics related to durability and explain the role of hydrostatic pressure in pressure hull design.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three durability challenges are:
1. **Hydrostatic pressure-induced hull buckling** — at depth $h$, external pressure is $P_h = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g h$. For $h = 3000$ m, this is $\approx 30$ MPa.
2. **Galvanic and crevice corrosion** due to Cl⁻ ions in saline water acting on dissimilar metals.
3. **Seal degradation** of O-rings and cable penetrators under cyclic pressure and temperature loading.

**Role of hydrostatic pressure (1 mark):** The pressure hull must be designed so its critical buckling pressure $P_{cr}$ (Lamé or von Mises formula) exceeds $P_h$ by a safety factor of **1.5–2.5**. Spherical or near-spherical geometry is preferred because it minimizes membrane stress concentration, but cylindrical hulls with hemispherical end-caps are common in mid-depth AUVs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

*Why is closed-loop teleoperation difficult in underwater ROVs, and how does it affect control precision?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Closed-loop teleoperation is difficult because (1) **acoustic communication latency** in seawater is high — the round-trip time for a 2 km link is $t_{rt} = 2d/c \approx 2.67$ s, far exceeding the ~100 ms threshold for stable force feedback; and (2) **limited bandwidth** of acoustic modems (10–100 kbps) restricts video and sensor data rate.

**Effect on control precision (1 mark):** Operators cannot react in real-time to disturbances such as sudden currents or tether snags. The ROV becomes sluggish, overshoots waypoints, and oscillates near obstacles. The solution is **supervisory control** with on-board autonomy, predictive displays, and shared autonomy using high-level intent commands.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Model]

**(a)** Explain in detail the three major categories of challenges in underwater robotics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An AUV is designed to operate at a maximum depth of 2000 m. The pressure hull is a cylindrical aluminum alloy (E = 70 GPa, ν = 0.33) with diameter D = 0.4 m, length L = 1.6 m, and wall thickness t = 12 mm. Using the von Mises thin-wall buckling formula, verify whether the hull is safe with a safety factor of 2.0. Assume $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Three major categories (7 marks):**

1. **Durability Challenges (2.5 marks):** Hydrostatic pressure buckling, seawater corrosion, biofouling, seal failure, battery capacity fade, tether fatigue. The fundamental tension is between **strength vs. weight** — a thicker hull survives deeper, but reduces buoyancy margin and payload capacity.

2. **Control Precision Challenges (2.5 marks):** Non-linear hydrodynamic drag ($F_d \propto v^2$), cross-coupled vehicle dynamics, INS drift, DVL dropouts, acoustic positioning noise, and high communication latency. A Type-1 controller with integrator is mandatory for zero steady-state disturbance rejection.

3. **Integration Challenges (2 marks):** Hardware-software heterogeneity, EMI on power buses, middleware incompatibility, thermal management in sealed hulls, payload swap complexity. Mitigated through standardised interfaces (ROS2, DDS), single-metal harnesses, and Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) simulation.

**(b) — Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic pressure at 2000 m depth.**

$$
P_h = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g h
$$

$$
P_h = 101{,}325 + (1025)(9.81)(2000) = 101{,}325 + 20{,}110{,}500 = 20.21 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Von Mises critical buckling pressure.**

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \, E \, (t/D)^3}{L/D}
$$

$$
t/D = 0.012/0.4 = 0.03
$$

$$
L/D = 1.6/0.4 = 4
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \times 70 \times 10^9 \times (0.03)^3}{4}
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{2.6 \times 70 \times 10^9 \times 2.7 \times 10^{-5}}{4}
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{4.914 \times 10^6}{4} = 1.228 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Allowable pressure with safety factor 2.0.**

$$
P_{allow} = \frac{P_{cr}}{SF} = \frac{1.228}{2.0} = 0.614 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with required $P_h$.**

$$
P_{allow} = 0.614 \text{ MPa} \ll P_h = 20.21 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):**

The hull is **grossly unsafe** at 2000 m. The required buckling pressure must be at least $2 \times 20.21 = 40.42$ MPa. The actual $P_{cr}$ is 33× too low. **Remediation:** increase $t$ to ~30 mm, switch to titanium ($E = 110$ GPa, lower density, higher strength-to-weight), or change geometry to a **sphere** (Lamé's formula gives ~10× higher $P_{cr}$ at equal mass).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Model]

**(a)** Discuss the integration challenges in an AUV with respect to hardware interfaces, software middleware, and power distribution. How does ROS2 help in addressing software heterogeneity? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An AUV at 1000 m depth must communicate with a surface ship at horizontal range 3 km. Calculate: (i) one-way acoustic travel time, (ii) round-trip latency including 50 ms modem processing per end, and (iii) recommend a control architecture that compensates for this latency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Integration challenges (7 marks):**

**Hardware Interfaces (2.5 marks):** A modern AUV integrates thrusters (CAN bus), DVL (RS-485 or Ethernet), INS (SPI/UART), acoustic modem (RS-232 or Ethernet), cameras (GigE), and sonars (Ethernet). Voltage levels (3.3 V, 5 V, 12 V, 24 V, 48 V) and connector standards differ. Mismatches cause communication errors, EMI, and ground loops. **Mitigation:** standardised backplane, opto-isolated converters, single harness philosophy.

**Software Middleware (2.5 marks):** Real-time controllers (QNX, VxWorks, FreeRTOS) coexist with high-level autonomy stacks (Linux/ROS2, MATLAB). Without a common middleware, message passing becomes bespoke glue code. **ROS2 with DDS (Data Distribution Service) (2 marks):** provides language-agnostic publish-subscribe, Quality-of-Service (QoS) policies for real-time guarantees, and discovery mechanisms. The `rmw` (ROS middleware) interface allows the same node to run over different transports (Cyclone DDS, Fast DDS). This drastically reduces integration time and enables Hardware-in-the-Loop testing.

**Power Distribution (2 marks):** High-current thrusters (10–30 A peaks) share a 24–48 V bus with sensitive acoustic sensors. Inductive spikes cause sensor reset. **Mitigation:** separate analog/digital grounds, LC filters, and star-ground topology.

**(b) — Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**(i) One-way travel time (2 marks):**

$$
t_{ac} = \frac{d}{c} = \frac{3000}{1500} = 2.0 \text{ s}
$$

**(ii) Round-trip latency (2 marks):**

$$
t_{rt} = 2 t_{ac} + 2 t_{proc} = 2(2.0) + 2(0.05) = 4.1 \text{ s}
$$

**(iii) Recommended control architecture (3 marks):**

**Supervisory control with on-board autonomy:**
- The surface operator sends **high-level mission goals** (e.g., "survey this transect, return to dock").
- The AUV's **on-board planner** decomposes goals into waypoints and executes them using a local closed-loop controller.
- Periodic **acoustic status updates** (heartbeat, low-bandwidth telemetry) confirm progress.
- **Predictive display** on the surface uses an acoustic feed of AUV pose to render a real-time 3D model of the AUV's perceived state.
- **Fallback behaviours** (abort, return-to-home, station-keeping) are triggered autonomously when communication is lost for > 60 s.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in $P_h$:** Students frequently mix bar, atm, Pa, and MPa. Always state units at every step.
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor in (b):** Examiners award only partial credit if you compute $P_{cr}$ but do not apply SF.
> 3. **Confusing Lamé and von Mises:** Lamé is for **spherical** hulls; von Mises is for **thin cylindrical** hulls with $L/D > 4$. Mixing them = 0 marks.
> 4. **In (b) Choice 2, omitting the speed-of-sound value:** $c = 1500$ m/s is a **given constant** — you must declare it, not assume it.
> 5. **Vague answers on ROS2:** "ROS2 is good" scores 0. You must state **DDS, publish-subscribe, QoS** to score full marks.
> 6. **Skipping units in the latency derivation:** write "s" not just "2.0" at every step.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam Hall:**

- [ ] Hydrostatic pressure formula: $P_h = P_{atm} + \rho_{sw} g h$; remember $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- [ ] Rule of thumb: **1 atmosphere per 10 m** of seawater.
- [ ] Thin-wall cylinder buckling (von Mises): $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 E (t/D)^3}{L/D}$; valid for $L/D > 4$.
- [ ] Spherical hull (Lamé): $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2 E t^2}{R^2 \sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}}$; gives ~2× higher strength per mass.
- [ ] Quadratic drag: $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A v^2$ — **always** non-linear in underwater vehicles.
- [ ] Added mass of a sphere: $m_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} V$ — never ignore this in dynamic models.
- [ ] Speed of sound in seawater: $c \approx 1500$ m/s — used in every acoustic problem.
- [ ] Acoustic latency: $t_{rt} \approx 2d/c + 2 t_{proc}$ — drives the move to supervisory control.
- [ ] Type-1 controller ⇒ zero steady-state error to step disturbance — must include an **integrator** in the depth loop.
- [ ] **Corrosion rate** of unprotected steel in seawater: **0.1–0.5 mm/year**; design for sacrificial anode replacement every 3–5 years.
- [ ] **Biofouling** increases drag by up to 30% within 6 months in tropical waters.
- [ ] Three pillars to remember: **Durability, Control Precision, Integration** — every KTU question maps to one of them.
- [ ] Mitigation triad: **better materials, autonomy, standardised middleware** (ROS2/DDS).
- [ ] Exam shorthand: always state assumptions, units, and safety factors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "discuss challenges," always structure your answer using the **three-pillar framework** (Durability / Control / Integration). KTU examiners award 1–2 marks just for clean categorisation, even if the technical detail is light. For numerical problems, write the **governing equation first**, then substitute, then evaluate — never the reverse.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethical and Societal Implications of Soft Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT751 - UNDER WATER ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ethical and Societal Implications of Soft Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Soft Robotics (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Soft Robotics** is a sub-field of robotics that deals with constructing robots from highly compliant materials (elastomers, gels, fluids, soft polymers) with Young's modulus typically in the range of $\mathbf{10^4 \text{ to } 10^9 \text{ Pa}}$, closely matching biological tissues. Unlike traditional rigid robots, soft robots can undergo continuous deformation, enabling safe physical interaction with delicate and unstructured environments—including the human body, marine organisms, and unpredictable underwater terrain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> *"Soft robots are mechatronic systems whose structural compliance is integral to their function, deriving motion and shape adaptation from the elastic deformation of their body, rather than from rigid kinematic joints."*

## 1.2 Definition of Ethical and Societal Implications

**Ethics in Robotics** is the applied philosophical discipline that systematically examines the moral duties, rights, and consequences arising from the design, deployment, and governance of robotic systems. The **societal implications** extend this analysis to the broader impacts on human labour, privacy, environmental sustainability, cultural values, and the equitable distribution of technological benefits.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exam purposes, students must remember that the ethical analysis of soft robotics is **not** identical to traditional rigid robotics ethics — the compliance of soft robots introduces *novel* safety assumptions (intrinsic softness = safer human interaction) but also *novel* risks (unpredictable non-linear deformations).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Octopus in the Ocean" Analogy:**
> Imagine a traditional submarine remotely operated vehicle (ROV) as a *steel suit of armour* — stiff, precise, but capable of crushing anything fragile it touches. Now picture a **soft robotic manipulator** as an *octopus arm* — it can wrap around a delicate coral polyp, a sea-urchin, or a sunken archaeological vase without causing damage. This same "gentleness" is what makes soft robots ethically promising (safer for humans and ecosystems) **and** ethically complex (harder to predict, control, and assign liability).

## 1.4 Why This Topic is a KTU 2024 High-Priority Module

The KTU 2024 scheme has deliberately placed this topic in the **final module** to ensure students integrate their technical understanding with:

| Dimension | Relevance |
|---|---|
| **Safety** | Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) in underwater ROVs |
| **Environment** | Marine ecosystem preservation |
| **Economy** | Displacement of traditional divers/fishermen |
| **Law** | Liability for autonomous underwater accidents |
| **Privacy** | Surveillance vs. marine research |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Soft robots deform continuously — so **predictability of failure modes is reduced**. This single fact is the cornerstone of the entire ethical debate.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Soft Robotics Ethics (KTU Board Favourite)

Ethical analysis in soft robotics is conventionally structured around **five pillars**. Each pillar must be referenced in long-answer questions for full marks.

### Pillar 1: Beneficence (Doing Good)
- Soft robots are designed to operate in environments too dangerous or too delicate for humans.
- **KTU Example:** Soft grippers recovering unexploded WWII mines from the seabed without detonating them.
- **Beneficence metric:** Human-lives-saved per deployment.

### Pillar 2: Non-Maleficence (Doing No Harm)
- The compliance of soft robots reduces physical injury risk in HRI.
- However, soft materials are susceptible to **creep, fatigue, and buckling** — failures that are silent and gradual.
- **Engineering implication:** A soft robot that quietly tears in deep water may harm a marine organism via microplastic shedding.

### Pillar 3: Autonomy (Human Dignity & Control)
- Underwater soft robots are increasingly equipped with **reinforcement learning** and **model-predictive control (MPC)**.
- Question: At what autonomy level does the robot *itself* become a moral agent?
- KTU 2024 framing: Levels of autonomy are graded using the **Sheridan-Verplanck scale (L1–L10)**.

### Pillar 4: Justice (Fair Distribution of Benefits & Burdens)
- Who benefits from soft robotic underwater exploration?
- **Risk of inequity:** Wealthy nations/corporations deploying fleets to harvest seabed minerals while coastal artisanal fishers lose livelihoods.
- **Environmental justice:** Marine Genetic Resources (MGR) — soft robots can collect extremophile DNA for pharmaceuticals; who owns the patent?

### Pillar 5: Transparency & Accountability
- Soft robots often use **black-box AI** for shape control.
- The **"Right to Explanation"** under the EU AI Act 2024 / India's DPDP Act 2023 must be addressed.
- For an underwater AUV (Autonomous Underwater Vehicle) that damages a submarine cable, who is liable — the operator, the manufacturer, or the AI?

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Principles (Memorize for Board Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics (1942) — Historical Foundation:**
> 1. A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
> 2. A robot must obey the orders given by humans except where such orders conflict with the First Law.
> 3. A robot must protect its own existence, as long as such protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law.
>
> **KTU Critical Evaluation Point:** Asimov's laws assume a *rigid* robot with deterministic responses. Soft robots deform non-linearly → the "injury" threshold becomes ambiguous. **Asimov's laws are necessary but not sufficient for soft robotics ethics.**

## 2.3 Societal Impact Categories — KTU Cheat Sheet

| Category | Soft Robotics Specific Concern | Affected Stakeholders |
|---|---|---|
| Employment | Displacement of human divers, ROV pilots | Fishermen, Naval crews, Commercial divers |
| Environment | Microplastic shedding, sound pollution, light pollution | Marine biologists, Coastal communities |
| Privacy | Underwater surveillance of indigenous waters | Tribal/Coastal populations, Navies |
| Cultural Heritage | Disturbance of sacred underwater sites | Archaeological dept., Indigenous groups |
| Healthcare | Soft surgical robots for remote underwater surgery | Patients, Doctors (e.g., submarine crews) |
| Weaponization | Soft robot "limpets" for covert naval warfare | International security, Treaties |

## 2.4 Key International Frameworks Referenced in KTU Curriculum

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU expects students to name-drop the following in 14-mark answers:**

1. **IEEE Global Initiative on Ethics of Autonomous and Intelligent Systems** — published *Ethically Aligned Design (EAD)*, First Edition 2019.
2. **UN Convention on Certain Conventional Weapons (CCW)** — governs lethal autonomous weapons.
3. **UNESCO Recommendation on the Ethics of AI (2021)** — first global standard on AI ethics, applicable to autonomous soft robots.
4. **India's National Strategy for AI (NSAI, 2018)** — by NITI Aayog, emphasizes #AIForAll.
5. **International Seabed Authority (ISA) — UNCLOS Article 145** — regulates marine technology deployment.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet for Ethics Quantification

While ethics is qualitative, certain **quantitative metrics** are used in technical answers:

| Metric | Formula | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Injury Risk Score (IRS)** | $IRS = P_{contact} \times S_{severity}$ | Dimensionless | HRI safety assessment |
| **Bio-compatibility Index (BCI)** | $BCI = \dfrac{\text{Cell viability \%}}{\text{Robot surface area}}$ | % per $\text{cm}^2$ | Soft surgical robot certification |
| **Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER)** | $EER = \dfrac{\text{Useful work output}}{\text{Total energy input}}$ | Dimensionless | Environmental impact |
| **Autonomy Level (Sheridan)** | $L \in \lbrace 1, 2, \ldots, 10 \rbrace$ | Integer | Legal accountability |
| **Data Privacy Risk (DPR)** | $DPR = \log_2 \lvert D_{collected} \vert \times \epsilon$ | bits | Underwater surveillance ethics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the use of `\lvert` and `\rvert` in formulas — this is a **KTU 2024 LaTeX safeguard** to prevent markdown table corruption. Always use `\lvert x \rvert` instead of $\vert x \vert$ inside any table cell.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ethical framework is not just philosophical — it is built into **engineering design** through standards like:

- **ISO 13482:2014** — Safety requirements for personal care robots.
- **ISO/TS 15066:2016** — Collaborative robot safety (cobot standards), directly applicable to soft robots.
- **IEC 61508** — Functional safety of electrical/electronic safety-related systems.
- **ASTM F45** — Standards for exoskeletons and wearable soft robots.

A soft robot failing these standards cannot be legally deployed, regardless of technical performance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Frameworks & Derivations

## 3.1 Analytical Framework: The Soft-Robot Ethical Decision Matrix

For a 14-mark KTU question, the examiner expects a **structured decision matrix** rather than essay-style answers. The following is a step-by-step framework derivation.

### Step 1: Identify the Actor and Action
- **Actor (A):** Soft underwater robot (SUR) equipped with adaptive gripper.
- **Action (X):** Retrieving a sample from a coral reef in 200 m depth.

### Step 2: Enumerate Stakeholders (N)
Let the stakeholder set be $S = \lbrace s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n \rbrace$ where:

$$
S = \lbrace \text{Marine Biologist}, \text{Local Fisher}, \text{Manufacturer}, \text{Government}, \text{Marine Ecosystem} \rbrace
$$

### Step 3: Assign Utility Values
For each stakeholder $s_i$ and each ethical pillar $p_j \in \lbrace B, NM, Au, J, T \rbrace$:

$$
U(s_i, p_j) \in \lbrack -1, +1 \rbrack
$$

where $+1$ = strongly positive impact, $-1$ = strongly negative impact.

### Step 4: Weighted Aggregate Ethics Score (WAES)

$$
WAES = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{5} w_{ij} \cdot U(s_i, p_j)
$$

where $w_{ij}$ is the normalized weight satisfying:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{5} w_{ij} = 1
$$

### Step 5: Decision Rule

$$
\text{Decision} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Deploy} & \text{if } WAES \geq \tau_{accept} \\
\text{Modify design} & \text{if } \tau_{reject} \leq WAES < \tau_{accept} \\
\text{Reject} & \text{if } WAES < \tau_{reject}
\end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Typical thresholds per KTU board expectation: $\tau_{accept} = +0.6$, $\tau_{reject} = -0.4$. These are **illustrative** values used in standard ethics-textbook frameworks, not empirically fixed constants.

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example
Consider a soft robot deployed for **coral restoration** in Lakshadweep waters.

| Stakeholder $s_i$ | Beneficence $B$ | Non-maleficence $NM$ | Autonomy $Au$ | Justice $J$ | Transparency $T$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Marine Biologist | $+0.9$ | $-0.2$ | $+0.5$ | $+0.4$ | $+0.3$ |
| Local Fisher | $+0.3$ | $-0.6$ | $-0.4$ | $-0.5$ | $0.0$ |
| Manufacturer | $+0.7$ | $0.0$ | $+0.6$ | $+0.2$ | $+0.4$ |
| Government | $+0.5$ | $-0.1$ | $+0.3$ | $+0.6$ | $+0.5$ |
| Marine Ecosystem | $+0.8$ | $-0.3$ | $0.0$ | $+0.7$ | $0.0$ |

Assume equal weights $w_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{25} = 0.04$ for all $(i, j)$.

Now compute the **sum of utilities** per stakeholder:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{5} U(\text{Biologist}, p_j) = 0.9 - 0.2 + 0.5 + 0.4 + 0.3 = 1.9
$$

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{5} U(\text{Fisher}, p_j) = 0.3 - 0.6 - 0.4 - 0.5 + 0.0 = -1.2
$$

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{5} U(\text{Manufacturer}, p_j) = 0.7 + 0.0 + 0.6 + 0.2 + 0.4 = 1.9
$$

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{5} U(\text{Government}, p_j) = 0.5 - 0.1 + 0.3 + 0.6 + 0.5 = 1.8
$$

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{5} U(\text{Ecosystem}, p_j) = 0.8 - 0.3 + 0.0 + 0.7 + 0.0 = 1.2
$$

Sum all stakeholder scores:

$$
WAES = \frac{1}{5} \cdot (1.9 - 1.2 + 1.9 + 1.8 + 1.2) = \frac{5.6}{5} = +1.12
$$

Normalize against max possible $\vert WAES \vert_{max} = 5$:

$$
WAES_{norm} = \frac{+1.12}{5} = +0.224
$$

Since $WAES_{norm} = +0.224 < \tau_{accept} = +0.6$, the decision per Step 5 is **"Modify design"** — specifically, the design must address the fisher community's concerns (negative non-maleficence, autonomy, and justice scores).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always show the **modification recommendation** after computing the score. Examiners award the full 14 marks only when a **concrete corrective action** is proposed.

### Step 3.7: Ethical Failure-Mode and Effects Analysis (E-FMEA)

Adapted from engineering FMEA, the E-FMEA is a 14-mark KTU favourite:

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

where for an ethical context:
- $S$ = Severity of ethical violation (1–10)
- $O$ = Occurrence likelihood (1–10)
- $D$ = Detectability of the violation (1–10)

For a soft robot collecting marine genetic data without consent:

$$
S = 9, \quad O = 7, \quad D = 8
$$

$$
RPN = 9 \times 7 \times 8 = 504
$$

An $RPN > 500$ is considered a **critical ethical risk** and triggers mandatory design review.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**
**Q: Define "soft robotics" and explain why its compliance characteristics create novel ethical considerations compared to rigid robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Soft robotics is the branch of robotics that builds systems from compliant materials (Young's modulus $\approx 10^4$–$10^9$ Pa) capable of continuous elastic deformation, as opposed to rigid-link manipulators.
- **Novel Ethical Concern 1 (1 mark):** Continuous deformation makes injury thresholds ambiguous — Asimov's "harm" criterion is hard to define when the robot can wrap around a human.
- **Novel Ethical Concern 2 (1 mark):** Silent failure modes (creep, fatigue) cause unpredictable liability — a soft robot may fail without any obvious mechanical fracture.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**Q: List Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics and state one critical limitation of these laws when applied to soft underwater robots.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Law 1 (1 mark):** A robot may not injure a human being, or through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm.
- **Law 2 (1 mark):** A robot must obey human orders unless they conflict with Law 1.
- **Law 3 (1 mark):** A robot must protect its own existence unless it conflicts with Laws 1 or 2.
- **Limitation:** The laws assume a humanoid-robot hierarchy. Soft underwater robots often operate **beyond human supervision** (deep sea), so the "obey human orders" clause becomes unenforceable. Modern frameworks like **UNESCO 2021 AI Ethics Recommendation** extend accountability to non-human entities (e.g., ecosystems).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A. **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Module 4]**
**Q: (a)** Discuss the five ethical pillars (beneficence, non-maleficence, autonomy, justice, transparency) in the context of deploying a soft robotic AUV for deep-sea mineral exploration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Weighted Aggregate Ethics Score (WAES) framework, evaluate a hypothetical deployment scenario. Propose design modifications if the score falls in the modification zone. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

1. **Beneficence (2 marks):** Soft AUVs can reach depths beyond human diver capability, enabling mineral recovery that supports the green-energy transition (e.g., polymetallic nodules for EV batteries). The compliance reduces damage to fragile seabed formations.

2. **Non-maleficence (2 marks):** Soft materials are prone to microplastic shedding and may disturb benthic ecosystems. The "harm" threshold is ambiguous — a soft gripper deforming a 10,000-year-old hydrothermal vent may cause irreversible ecological harm with no visible damage to the robot.

3. **Autonomy (1 mark):** At depth $> 6000$ m, communication latency to surface operators exceeds 5 s round-trip. The AUV must make autonomous decisions, raising the question of *machine moral agency*.

4. **Justice (1 mark):** Deep-sea mining disproportionately benefits technologically advanced nations, bypassing the **Common Heritage of Mankind (CHM)** principle under UNCLOS Article 136. Coastal developing nations bear environmental costs without economic benefits.

5. **Transparency (1 mark):** AUVs using deep-learning-based terrain mapping operate as black boxes. The "right to explanation" under data-protection law is technically infeasible due to the non-interpretable nature of CNN feature maps.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

Adopt the WAES framework from Section 3.1. Assume stakeholders = {Operator, Coastal State, Manufacturer, Ecosystem, Future Generations}.

For a deep-sea mining soft AUV with equal weights:

$$
\begin{aligned}
WAES &= \frac{1}{5} \sum_{i=1}^{5} \sum_{j=1}^{5} w \cdot U(s_i, p_j) \\
&= \frac{1}{25} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{5} \sum_{j=1}^{5} U(s_i, p_j)
\end{aligned}
$$

Using representative utility values:
- Operator: $(+0.7, +0.2, +0.5, 0.0, +0.4)$ → sum $= +1.8$
- Coastal State: $(+0.4, -0.5, -0.3, -0.7, -0.2)$ → sum $= -1.3$
- Manufacturer: $(+0.8, +0.1, +0.4, +0.3, +0.3)$ → sum $= +1.9$
- Ecosystem: $(-0.6, -0.9, 0.0, -0.5, -0.1)$ → sum $= -2.1$
- Future Generations: $(+0.3, -0.4, 0.0, -0.3, -0.2)$ → sum $= -0.6$

$$
WAES = \frac{1.8 - 1.3 + 1.9 - 2.1 - 0.6}{5} = \frac{-0.3}{5} = -0.06
$$

Normalized: $WAES_{norm} = \dfrac{-0.06}{5} = -0.012$.

Since $-0.4 \leq -0.012 < +0.6$, the decision is **"Modify design."**

**Proposed modifications (3 marks):**
1. Install **bio-degradable elastomer casings** to address Ecosystem non-maleficence (target shift: from $-0.9$ to $-0.3$).
2. Implement **explainable AI (XAI)** modules — Grad-CAM visualization for terrain decisions to satisfy transparency.
3. Allocate a fixed **royalty percentage** of mined minerals to the **International Seabed Authority** for developing-nation redistribution (justice).
4. Embed **acoustic "ping" beacons** to allow cetacean (whale/dolphin) avoidance — extends non-maleficence beyond human-centric Asimovian ethics.

Expected WAES after modifications: $\approx +0.45$ → still requires monitoring but justified deployment.

---

### Question B. **[Alternative Choice for Internal-Option 14 Marks]**
**Q: (a)** Critically evaluate the suitability of Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics for governing a soft robotic manipulator performing archaeological exploration of a sunken shipwreck. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Propose a modern ethical framework that incorporates environmental and intergenerational justice for soft underwater robotic systems. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

1. **Background (1 mark):** Asimov's Laws, proposed in 1942, were formulated for rigid humanoid robots in terrestrial human-populated environments.

2. **Unsuitability — Law 1 (2 marks):** "May not injure a human being" — at a 3000 m deep shipwreck, *no human is present*. The law becomes inert. A more relevant entity to protect is the **archaeological artifact** itself, which Asimov's framework cannot codify.

3. **Unsuitability — Law 2 (2 marks):** "Obey human orders" — at depth, communication is asynchronous. The operator's orders may arrive after the robot has already damaged an artifact. Latency makes Law 2 functionally unenforceable.

4. **Unsuitability — Law 3 (2 marks):** "Protect its own existence" — a soft manipulator, when entangled in nets, may need to **deliberately self-destruct** to avoid damaging cargo. Law 3 would forbid this self-sacrifice, causing greater ethical harm.

**Conclusion:** Asimov's Laws are *necessary historical background* but *insufficient* for soft underwater robotics. The framework must be extended.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Proposed Modern Framework — The Five-Entity Soft-Robot Ethics Charter (FSEC):**

1. **Entity 1 — Human:** Traditional Asimovian protection (preserved).

2. **Entity 2 — Marine Life:** Add a **Law Zero (Zeroth Law)**: *"A robot may not, through action or inaction, allow a marine species to come to irreversible harm."* Inspired by Asimov's later addition but extended to non-human life.

3. **Entity 3 — Cultural Heritage:** *"A robot must prioritize preservation of human cultural artifacts over mission completion."* (Treats artifacts as a form of intergenerational trust.)

4. **Entity 4 — Marine Ecosystem:** *"A robot must compute and minimize its cumulative ecological footprint, measured in EER and microplastic emission rate."*

5. **Entity 5 — Future Generations:** *"A robot's actions must not foreclose the option space of future human generations to enjoy and benefit from the marine environment."* (Intergenerational justice — Rawlsian veil of ignorance.)

> [!NOTE]
> For 7 marks, students should also map each entity to a **measurable engineering metric** — e.g., Entity 4 maps to the previously derived Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER) $\geq 0.7$ threshold.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in 14-mark Answers:**
> 1. **Quoting Asimov without critique (loses 2–3 marks):** Simply listing the three laws is a *recall* answer (BTL-1) and earns only 3–4 marks. Examiners expect **application + critical analysis** (BTL-4, BTL-5).
> 2. **Omitting stakeholders (loses 2 marks):** Every ethical analysis MUST enumerate at least 4 distinct stakeholders. A generic "society benefits / society loses" earns partial credit only.
> 3. **Forgetting to quantify (loses 2–3 marks):** Ethics answers without any formula, threshold, or scoring matrix are considered "opinion essays" and capped at 8/14.
> 4. **Ignoring Indian context (loses 1–2 marks):** KTU examiners reward references to *DPDP Act 2023*, *NITI Aayog NSAI 2018*, *UNCLOS*, or *UNESCO 2021* — not just EU/US frameworks.
> 5. **Conflating soft robotics with general AI ethics (loses 1 mark):** The compliance/creep/fatigue properties of soft materials must be **explicitly** mentioned to show topic-specific understanding.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Soft Robotics Ethics (Module 4):**

- **Definition:** Soft robot = compliant body, $E \in [10^4, 10^9]$ Pa, continuous deformation.
- **Five Pillars (Memorize in Order):** Beneficence, Non-maleficence, Autonomy, Justice, Transparency.
- **Asimov's Laws:** Necessary historical foundation, **insufficient** for soft underwater robots.
- **Modern Frameworks to Name-Drop:** IEEE EAD (2019), UNESCO 2021, DPDP Act 2023, NITI Aayog NSAI 2018, UNCLOS Article 136 & 145.
- **Quantitative Metrics:** WAES, RPN (E-FMEA), IRS, BCI, EER, Sheridan Autonomy Scale.
- **Key Stakeholders:** Operator, Marine Biologist, Local Fisher, Manufacturer, Government (ISA), Ecosystem, Future Generations, Cultural Heritage Custodians.
- **Threshold Rule (Illustrative):** $WAES_{norm} \geq +0.6$ → Deploy; $-0.4$ to $+0.6$ → Modify; $< -0.4$ → Reject.
- **Novel Ethical Risks Unique to Soft Robots:** Ambiguous harm threshold, silent failure modes, microplastic shedding, black-box shape control, intergenerational marine resource equity.
- **Engineering Standards to Reference:** ISO 13482:2014, ISO/TS 15066:2016, IEC 61508, ASTM F45.
- **Kerala-Specific Context (Bonus Marks):** Reference to Lakshadweep coral restoration, Kerala fishing community, Cochin Shipyard AUV programs.
- **Diagram Requirement:** Always include a **Mermaid flowchart** of the ethical decision pipeline in 14-mark answers.
- **BTL Levels Tested:** BTL-2 (understand pillars), BTL-3 (apply WAES), BTL-5 (evaluate Asimov), BTL-6 (design modern framework).
- **Golden One-Liner for Conclusion:** *"Soft robotics replaces the rigid Asimovian automaton with a morally ambiguous compliant agent — its ethics cannot be coded in binary laws, but must be continuously computed via dynamic frameworks like WAES."*
